meta.title: Mercedes-AMG M176/M177 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
meta.description: The M176/M177 is a potent 4.0L AMG V8 — but hot-vee design means oil leaks and cam wear are common. See honest replacement costs and compare specialist quotes.

mainEntity.name: How much does an M176/M177 engine replacement cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M176/M177 typically costs £7,000–£12,000 supply only, varying by specific variant and output. Fitting adds £1,500–£3,000 depending on labour rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-AMG M176/M177 a reliable engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M176/M177 is rated 3.5★ out of 5 — generally robust for a high-performance V8, but its hot-vee design creates heat-related maintenance demands. Many specialists recommend major inspection around 60,000–90,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the most common M176/M177 failure?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common issues include camshaft wear (ticking noise, Check Engine Light codes P0016/P0017), oil leaks from camshaft position sensors and adjuster magnets, rear main seal failure from blocked oil-vapour separators, and early-production ignition coil misfires.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes models use the M176/M177?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M176 powers non-AMG V8 models like the G 500/G 550 and S 560/S 580. The M177 powers AMG models including the C 63, E 63, G 63, GLC 63, and GT 63, plus Aston Martin Vantage and DB11.

mainEntity.name: How long does the M176/M177 last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance, these engines can reach 90,000–150,000 miles. However, specialists note that major overhauls are common in this mileage range — strict servicing and early attention to oil leaks are essential for longevity.

hero.h1: Mercedes-AMG M176/M177 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes-AMG M176/M177 is a 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 — a potent, hand-built performance engine, but its hot-vee design creates heat-related maintenance demands. Backed by real UK enquiries from EnginesMarket.co.uk.

rows.value: Also fitted to Aston Martin Vantage, DB11, DBX

rows.metric: Confidence: 88% confidence — based on verified specialist rebuild reports and UK workshop consensus.

rows.value: Mix of cheap fixes (oil leaks, sensors) and expensive ones (camshaft, RMS)

rows.metric: Available but specialist-order only; AMG parts premium applies

rows.value: Recon cost 45–59% of lowest host-vehicle value
One-line verdict: The M176/M177 is a phenomenal performer when maintained — but its hot-vee design means oil leaks and camshaft wear are common from 60,000 miles — budget for specialist maintenance.

rows.metric: Best for: Enthusiasts who want AMG V8 performance and can afford specialist maintenance.
rows.value: Avoid if: You're on a tight budget or looking at a high-mileage example with no documented service history.
AMG GT / GT S / GT C (M178)
Also powers the Aston Martin Vantage (2018–present), DB11 V8 (2017–2023), and DBX (2020–present).
Labour estimate: Fitting an M176/M177 typically takes 12–18 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer).

rows.metric: Shared-cost note: Parts cost is identical across Mercedes-AMG and Aston Martin applications; labour time varies slightly by engine bay access.
